sanskrit_devanagari:
यदा संहरते चायं कूर्मोऽङ्गानीव सर्वशः ।
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ॥ ५८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yadā saṁharate cāyaṁ kūrmo 'ṅgānīva sarvaśaḥ |
indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyas tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā || 58 ||

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.058 = MBh_06,024.058). Body text identical: 'yadā saṃharate cāyaṃ kūrmo'ṅgānīva sarvaśaḥ | indriyāṇīndriyārthebhyastasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā'. GRETIL uses anusvara 'saṃharate' where modern editions print 'saṁharate'; both transcribe the same Devanagari conjunct.

translations.verbatim_quote: One who is able to withdraw his senses from sense objects, as the tortoise draws its limbs within the shell, is firmly fixed in perfect consciousness.
One who is able to withdraw the senses from their objects, just as a tortoise withdraws its limbs into its shell, is established in divine wisdom.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The test of a yogī, devotee or self-realized soul is that he is able to control the senses according to his plan. Most people, however, are servants of the senses and are thus directed by the dictation of the senses. That is the answer to the question as to how the yogī is situated.

And besides, yadā, when; ayam, this one, the sannyāsin practising steadfastness in Knowledge; saṁharate, fully withdraws ('Fully' suggests absolute firmness in withdrawal, and 'withdraws' suggests full control over the organs); indriyāṇi, the senses; indriyārthebhyaḥ, from all the objects of the senses; iva, as; kūrmaḥ, a tortoise; sarvaśaḥ, wholly (withdraws); aṅgāni, its limbs, from all sides out of fear — when the man engaged in steadfastness to Knowledge withdraws thus, then tasya, his; prajñā, wisdom; pratiṣṭhitā, remains established.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as a strict gloss on voluntary withdrawal: saṁharate = 'fully withdraws'; the prefix sam- conveys absolute firmness; the verb-stem hṛ conveys full control over the organs. The tortoise simile is a deliberate technical analogy — the sage is one who can withdraw the senses on demand, the way the tortoise pulls in its limbs. Shankara also flags a critical caveat (in his lead-in to 2.59): the sense-organs of even a sick or restrained person can withdraw mechanically; the difference is that for the sthita-prajña, the hankering also goes away (the topic of the next verse).

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: page returned identical text to Shankara excerpt (upstream data error on bhagavad-gita.us); Ramanuja's actual bhāṣya not separately captured for this verse.
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: [The bhagavad-gita.us page rendered the same Sanskrit-glossed translation under both Shankara and Ramanuja headings on this verse — an upstream data error. Ramanuja's bhāṣya on 2.58 emphasises (per Vishishtadvaita tradition) that voluntary sense-withdrawal is the lower stage of yoga; the higher stage requires that the mind itself be fixed on the Supreme. The taste-removal that happens by realisation is described in 2.59.]
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Source-pack honesty note: bhagavad-gita.us returned identical text under Shankara and Ramanuja headings for this verse, which appears to be a JS-rendering error in the source page. The summary stated above is from the standard Vishishtadvaita reading (cross-reference: Adidevananda translation of Ramanuja-Gita-Bhāṣya, which we have not pulled raw). Treat this entry as paraphrase-only; not as verbatim Ramanuja.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Sri Madhvacharya did not comment on this sloka.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Madhva's bhāṣya does not comment on 2.58 directly per the bhagavad-gita.us record. The Dvaita commentary tradition typically reads the sense-withdrawal here as a precondition for the focused devotion that follows.

positions.rendering: is able to withdraw (modal: ability to withdraw)
is able to withdraw (same modal reading)
fully withdraws — sam- = 'absolute firmness in withdrawal', verb-stem = 'full control over the organs'

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit verb saṁharate is in the indicative — 'when [the sage] withdraws'. The modern translators add a modal sense ('is able to withdraw') that softens the picture into 'capacity'. Shankara reads it as actual withdrawal-on-demand — the sage withdraws, period; the prefix sam- intensifies. The engineering layer should preserve the action, not just the capacity: the verse describes a sage who actually withdraws, not just one who could in principle.

positions.rendering: as the tortoise draws its limbs within the shell
just as a tortoise withdraws its limbs into its shell
the tortoise wholly withdraws its limbs from all sides out of fear

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The Sanskrit names a complete simile: tortoise / limbs / wholly / from all sides. Shankara adds 'out of fear' — the tortoise's natural reflex is fear-driven, and this is part of the analogy. The modern translators introduce the 'shell' which is implicit, not in the Sanskrit text. The verse text itself does not say 'shell' — it says 'wholly, from all sides'. The engineering layer should keep the structural point of the simile (voluntary, complete withdrawal of the operator's attention-organs) and not get attached to the shell-iconography.

literal_meaning: When this one withdraws the senses entirely from sense-objects, like a tortoise its limbs from all sides — their wisdom is established.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This is the famous tortoise verse — the most quoted simile in Chapter 2's sthita-prajña block. It names the sthita-prajña by a single capacity: voluntary, complete, on-demand withdrawal of the senses (indriyāṇi) from the objects of the senses (indriyārthebhyaḥ). The simile is precise: as the tortoise wholly withdraws its limbs from every side, the sage withdraws the attention-organs from every direction. Shankara emphasises that the prefix sam- conveys 'absolute firmness'; the verb conveys 'full control'. The verse describes not the absence of senses (the sage is not numb) but the operator's capacity to deploy and withdraw attention voluntarily. Critically, the next verse (2.59) will specify that this withdrawal is incomplete unless the taste (rasa) for sense-objects also goes away — which only happens upon seeing the Supreme. So 2.58 names a real but partial achievement; 2.59 names what completes it.
